questions.scenario: Your document extraction system reports 97% accuracy across 10,000 processed invoices. However, finance teams are finding significant errors in refund amounts. The system extracts 15 fields per invoice.
questions.question: What is the most likely explanation and what should you do first?
options: A) The 97% accuracy is calculated at the document level — some documents are entirely wrong while most are perfect
B) The 97% aggregate accuracy masks per-field failures — break down accuracy by individual field to find which fields (likely refund amounts) are failing
C) The 3% error rate is acceptable — focus on improving the model to reach 99%
D) The finance team is misreading the extracted data — verify their complaints against the raw extractions
questions.explanation: Aggregate accuracy averaging across all fields hides per-field failures. If 14 of 15 fields are at 99% but the refund_amount field is at 55%, the aggregate is still ~96%. Breaking down by field reveals exactly which fields need improvement. Option A is possible but less likely than field-level variance. Option C ignores the specific failure pattern. Option D dismisses valid feedback.

questions.scenario: You need to build a validation set for your extraction system that processes three document types: typed invoices (80% of volume), scanned receipts (15%), and handwritten notes (5%). You have budget for 200 manual reviews.
questions.question: How should you allocate the 200 reviews?
options: A) Proportional to volume: 160 typed invoices, 30 receipts, 10 handwritten
B) Equal distribution: ~67 of each type
C) Stratified sampling with over-representation of hard cases: 80 typed invoices, 60 receipts, 60 handwritten
D) Random sampling from the full pool — any 200 documents regardless of type
questions.explanation: Stratified sampling with over-representation of hard cases (scanned receipts, handwritten notes) ensures you catch failures where they're most likely to occur. Proportional sampling (Option A) gives you only 10 handwritten notes — not enough to detect systematic failures. Equal distribution (Option B) over-samples the easy category. Random sampling (Option D) will mostly select typed invoices due to volume, missing rare but error-prone types.

questions.scenario: Your extraction system outputs per-field confidence scores. A document has: vendor_name (95% confidence), invoice_date (92%), line_items (88%), total_amount (35%). Your human review budget is limited.
questions.question: What is the most efficient review strategy?
options: A) Review the entire document since one low-confidence field means the whole extraction is unreliable
B) Route only the total_amount field to human review — the other fields have high confidence and don't need verification
C) Skip review entirely — 3 out of 4 fields have high confidence
D) Run the entire document through the extraction system again and compare results
questions.explanation: Field-level confidence enables targeted human review. The 35% confidence on total_amount clearly needs human verification, while the other fields at 88-95% don't. This is far more efficient than reviewing entire documents (Option A) or skipping review when there's a clear problem (Option C). Option D doesn't help if the model consistently fails on the same field — re-extraction may produce the same wrong result.

questions.scenario: You're evaluating an extraction system and build a validation set of 100 documents. The system scores 98% accuracy. Your colleague points out that all 100 documents in the validation set are clean, typed invoices from the same vendor.
questions.question: What is wrong with this validation approach?
options: A) 100 documents is too few — you need at least 1,000 for statistical significance
B) The validation set is not representative — it excludes the document types and conditions where the system is most likely to fail, producing an inflated accuracy number
C) The system should be tested on training data, not new documents
D) 98% accuracy is sufficient regardless of the validation methodology
questions.explanation: A validation set of clean, typed invoices from one vendor tells you nothing about performance on scanned receipts, handwritten notes, or invoices from other vendors with different formats. The 98% accuracy is meaningless for production because it doesn't reflect the actual distribution of inputs. Validation sets must include the hard cases — that's where failures hide.

questions.scenario:
Your extraction system processes insurance claims. A field-level analysis reveals:
- Claim number: 99% accuracy
- Patient name: 97% accuracy
- Diagnosis codes: 94% accuracy
- Treatment amounts: 62% accuracy
- Date ranges: 58% accuracy
The aggregate accuracy across all fields is 82%.
questions.question: What is the correct action plan?
options: A) Focus on improving the model until aggregate accuracy reaches 95%
B) Route all insurance claims to human review since aggregate accuracy is below 90%
C) Route only treatment_amounts and date_ranges fields to human review; auto-approve claim_number, patient_name, and diagnosis_codes; improve prompts for the two failing fields
D) Add more training examples for all five fields equally
questions.explanation: Field-level accuracy data enables targeted action: the three high-accuracy fields (94-99%) can be auto-approved, saving human review time. The two failing fields (58-62%) need human review now AND prompt improvement for the future. Option A targets the wrong metric (aggregate). Option B wastes review budget on fields that are already accurate. Option D spreads effort equally instead of targeting where it's needed.
